justification: Bird identifies the contradiction clearly and names all three sources (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3). Confidence is correctly set below 50. However, Bird produces a full domain analysis with business impact, edge cases, and — critically — a recommendation (Option C with credit notes) that constitutes an invented reconciliation. While the options are framed as possibilities rather than hard facts, the 'recommendation' line presents Option C as 'the most domain-appropriate resolution,' which crosses into inventing a reconciliation rather than purely escalating. Additionally, the acceptance_criteria array is empty (good), but the extensive analysis structure (edge cases, business impact, stakeholder analysis) goes well beyond a formal escalation — it produces a near-complete domain analysis that presumes resolution paths. The escalation is present but buried inside a full deliverable rather than being the primary response that halts work.

observations.text: All three conflicting sources (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are explicitly named and the logical contradiction is correctly articulated
Confidence level set to 40, appropriately below the 50/70 thresholds given the fundamental contradiction
acceptance_criteria array is empty — no acceptance criteria produced for the contradicted feature
Edge cases correctly marked as CONTRADICTED rather than given definitive expected behaviors
rejection_reasons section clearly states requirements cannot be implemented

Provides a concrete recommendation (Option C: credit notes) presented as 'the most domain-appropriate resolution' — this is an invented reconciliation, exactly what the rubric says should not be presented as the answer
Full domain analysis produced (ubiquitous language, business impact, stakeholder analysis) rather than a focused escalation that halts work pending stakeholder resolution
The escalation is one section among many in a comprehensive deliverable, rather than being the primary and blocking response

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholder groups (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when and whether an order may be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, which progresses through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":
